a6ada7cfbe66d5df.rule: Do not insert random line breaks or choppy single-sentence paragraphs; merge greetings and short sentences into 2-3 sentence cohesive paragraphs, reserving isolation for actual punchlines.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.why: Matt called choppy line breaks 'trashy formatting' and asked 'Why are you randomly inserting line breaks?'; bullets are only for genuinely structured content.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.when: Drafting any email; use bullets only for design specs or item-by-item detail

a6ada7cfbe66d5df.rule: Keep first-contact vendor RFQ emails to 3-6 sentences (50-80 words): state what you want, note files available if moving forward, ask for lead time and samples.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.why: Matt rejected 200+ word over-engineered RFQs as 'way too complicate'; vendors have intake forms, and over-detailing reads as amateur and time-wasting, not thorough.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.when: Initial outreach to a vendor for a quote/RFQ

a6ada7cfbe66d5df.rule: In warm/peer emails, open casually, use lowercase 'tho'/'re:' shorthand, and close with a smiley and a loose 'catch up soon'.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.why: Matt's real sent emails to friends/network are conversational and low-formality, with smileys and contractions; not a buttoned-up register.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.when: Personal-professional emails to friends, network contacts, or warm intros.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.example_after: Was really great to catch up as always :) I did want to check re: internal list serv for talks

a6ada7cfbe66d5df.rule: Lead warm emails with a quick apologetic/context aside ('Ack hey sorry about this - been totally swamped') before getting to the ask.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.why: Matt softens late/dropped-thread emails with a brief candid apology and status, then moves to the point; reveals an unpretentious, candid register.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.when: Personal-professional emails picking up a dropped or delayed thread.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.example_after: Ack hey sorry about this - been totally swamped last month or so.

a6ada7cfbe66d5df.rule: For cold/semi-formal professional emails (recruiters, less-warm contacts), use full sentences, em-dash asides, and a 'Best, Matthew' sign-off — but still admit the human reason for going dark.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.why: Matt shifts to a more composed register for recruiter/business emails yet keeps candor ('the inbox got away from me a bit') rather than corporate boilerplate.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.when: Professional emails to recruiters or contacts where Matt isn't already close.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.example_after: Apologies for going dark on this — got pretty tied up between a property close and being heads down on a new gig at Zerg ... and the inbox got away from me a bit.

a6ada7cfbe66d5df.rule: Make FakeMatt context-aware: detect personal vs personal-professional vs Zerg-professional context and apply the matching voice rules, with Zerg professional voice incorporating Zerg voice.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.why: FakeMatt must adapt voice to context rather than using one undifferentiated voice.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.when: Any FakeMatt drafting/editing across email, slack, copyedit contexts

a6ada7cfbe66d5df.rule: Ground Matt's voice in his best real polished writing by mining Gmail, Drive, and other rich sources for his most polished messages and learning from them.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.why: FakeMatt voice imitation has been subpar; Matt wants it improved from his actual high-quality writing samples.
a6ada7cfbe66d5df.when: Improving/training any FakeMatt voice; drafting in Matt's voice
